name: Jack Teagarden
abstract: Weldon Leo "Jack" Teagarden (August 20, 1905 – January 15, 1964) was an American jazz trombonist and singer. He led both of his bands himself and was a sideman for Paul Whiteman's orchestra.

concerts.label: Fred Robbins' One-Night Stand: Louis Armstrong

works.t: No No Nanette: Tea for Two

about_quotes.speaker: James Chirillo
about_quotes.evidence: If Red Allen did it, that’s serious — Jack Teagarden, and the list just goes on and on and on. And those guys, those were the guys that defined everything that we’re doing today.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Chirillo interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: James Lincoln Collier
about_quotes.evidence: we were listening to the Ben Pollock Orchestra, you know, the band with Teagarden and Goodman... with Teagarden... These were the heroes.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Lincoln Collier interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Ed Shaughnessy Part I
about_quotes.evidence: the second guy that gave me a job was Jack Teagarden... would you like to work with me? I said “oh Jack, I’d love to work with you.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Ed Shaughnessy Part I interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 1, 1995 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Bruce Johnstone
about_quotes.evidence: I was listening to a lot of Dixieland as well, some Louis Armstrong, Jack Teagarden, and Miff Mole and his Little Molars.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bruce Johnstone interviewed by Monk Rowe, Dunkirk, New York, October 11, 2013

about_quotes.speaker: William Randolph "Cozy" Cole
about_quotes.evidence: Sid Catlett, Jack Teagarden, Earl Hines, Barney Bigard, and Louie, they were sensational... I was with him five years.

about_quotes.speaker: Akiyoshi Toshiko
about_quotes.evidence: There was a trombone player, played a little bit like Jack Teagarden, and tried to sing like that, too.
about_quotes.doc_title: Akiyoshi Toshiko — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
